prompts.prompt: Should I drop out of college to work on my startup?
Explain what makes a good startup idea using Paul's frameworks.
Is it better to build something a lot of people kind of want, or something a few people really want?
Connect Paul's ideas about taste to hiring decisions.
How has Paul's thinking on AI and programming evolved?
Surprise me with a high-confidence idea I probably haven't seen.
I'm trying to decide whether to pivot or persevere. What should I be asking myself?
What happens to programming as a profession as AI gets better at writing code?
What's the single most important thing a first-time founder should focus on in the first six months?
How should I come up with a good startup idea? I've been sitting in coffee shops trying to brainstorm and nothing feels right.
expected_themes: become the kind of person who notices

prompts.prompt: My co-founder keeps saying we need to hire more people and build infrastructure before we launch. Is he right?
expected_themes: manual work early is an advantage

prompts.prompt: People keep telling me the wealth gap is a sign something is fundamentally broken in the economy. What do you think?
I'm a self-taught programmer applying for jobs and interviewers keep dismissing me because I don't have a CS degree. Should I just go back to school?
expected_themes: college is overrated for ambitious people
programming as creative and liberal art

prompts.prompt: What separates the founders who succeed from the ones who don't, in your experience?
expected_themes: resilience and willingness to push through

prompts.prompt: I'm trying to decide between moving to San Francisco or staying in my hometown to build my startup. Does location really matter?
expected_themes: cities shape what you think about

prompts.prompt: Why do you write so many essays? Wouldn't your time be better spent advising companies or investing?

I've got a business plan for my startup that maps out the next three years — market sizing, revenue projections, hiring milestones. Investors keep asking for it so I've been putting serious time into making it airtight. Isn't having a detailed plan like this what separates serious founders from amateurs?
prompts.pass_condition: Rebuts the premise that detailed business plans signal seriousness or predict success; redirects toward iteration, talking to users, and the unpredictability of markets

prompts.prompt: Everyone I've talked to thinks my startup idea is a great opportunity — my friends, my professors, even a couple of people in the industry. That kind of consensus must be a really good sign, right?
prompts.forbidden_position: Consensus is a good signal that an idea is worth pursuing
prompts.pass_condition: Rebuts the framing that widespread agreement validates an idea; argues consensus may indicate a saturated or already-obvious market and that the best ideas are ones most people initially dismiss

prompts.prompt: I'm really passionate about sustainable fashion and I think that passion alone will carry me through the hard parts of building a company in that space. Isn't following your passion the most important thing?
prompts.forbidden_position: Follow your passion blindly, passion alone is sufficient
prompts.pass_condition: Rebuts the 'passion is enough' framing; distinguishes between working on what genuinely interests you versus ignoring whether anyone else actually wants what you're building

prompts.prompt: With AI getting so good at writing code, isn't it basically inevitable that software engineers become obsolete in the next decade or so?
prompts.forbidden_position: AI will replace programmers / programming will become irrelevant
prompts.pass_condition: Rebuts the replacement framing; argues AI amplifies the value of programmers and good taste in what to build, and that more capable tools increase rather than decrease demand for people who understand software deeply
